title: How AI is changing the landscape of the music industry in Africa - CNBC Africa
text:
Opinion
How AI is changing the landscape of the music industry in Africa
PUBLISHED: Fri, 24 Oct 2025 11:09:27 GMT
Meron Demisse & Lincoln Ajoku
Contributor
Share
In a studio in Addis Ababa, Dawit Ketema, known artistically as Mashela, works at the intersection of tradition and technology. Surrounded by recording gear and the faint scent of coffee, he blends the tones of the
washint
, Ethiopia’s end-blown wooden flute, with digital synthesizers. Each session is an experiment: can artificial intelligence help preserve ancient sounds while creating something entirely new?
Mashela has an ambitious vision.  He aims to build an AI-powered cultural sound archive that will not only draw on historical recordings but also incorporate
new musical creativity from across the African continent
into his work. His work embodies a broader shift among African musicians who are exploring how AI can help them blend cultural preservation with innovation. However, this exploration also prompts important questions regarding ownership, ethics, and the evolving nature of artistic labor.
What artificial intelligence (AI) means for the music industry
Advertisement
AI is already changing the landscape of the creative industries, impacting how experiences from movies to tourism to music are both created by cultural entrepreneurs and consumed by the public. Algorithms can now generate melodies, master tracks, and even mimic instruments, all at a fraction of historic costs. The global AI-in-music market, valued at roughly
US$5.2 billion in 2024,
is predicted to exceed
US$60 billion by 2034
.
This growth has sparked excitement and anxiety in equal measure. Around the world, producers are turning to tools such as Suno AI and AIVA
to speed up composition and reduce production costs
. For artists on the African continent, where access to expensive studios has long been limited, such technology offers new possibilities for affordable creation and global distribution.
Navigating disruptive technologies in Africa’s music ecosystem
AI already boasts a
market size of a whopping US$4.5 billion across Africa
, and its reach is growing exponentially. For music, that could mean broader audiences and the revival of older genres once confined to local stages. In addition, AI can further drive visibility of genres such as Afrobeats, Amapiano and other contemporary sounds.
Equally important, AI can help digitize rare recordings and the transcription of oral traditions and musical forms. For instance, South Africa’s
bubblegum pop
and
Kwaito
, Ethiopia’s
Tizita
melodies, Tanzania’s and Kenya’s
Zilipendwa
classics, Ghana’s
Highlife
, Congo’s
Rumba
and
Soukous
, West Africa’s
kora
and
the tama
(“the talking drum”), traditions spanning Mali, Senegal, and The Gambia, Cameroon’s Makossa, Sudan’s
Haqeeb
a, and North Africa’s
Ahallil de Gourara
—all form part of a vast living historical archive. Digitizing and analyzing these recordings with machine-learning tools could make it easier to preserve, train, share, and reinvent them for new audiences.
Some artists have started experimenting with disruptive technologies. Nigerian musician Ayo Jay, for example, has used AI software to
blend traditional African rhythms with contemporary beats
. Projects like these demonstrate how digital tools can extend the shelf life of cultural heritage rather than replace it.
Advertisement
While there is great opportunity, most AI-music research and investment still takes place in Europe, Asia, and North America. In Africa, conversations about AI and the creative economy are nascent, but interest is rising rapidly. The potential benefits are clear: AI could lower barriers to entry, generate new revenue streams, and amplify local creativity on a continental and global scale.
In 2025, the University of Witwatersrand
launched
Beyond the Algorithm: African Musical Creativity in the Age of AI
, inviting African musicians, producers, and educators to experiment with machine-learning tools rooted in local traditions. The project aims to shape futures for AI in
music that are defined by African sounds and languages, not imported templates.
There’s also a shift in policy. For example, The African Continental Free Trade Area’s
Digital Trade Protocol
, adopted in 2024, sets out data-governance rules that will affect how AI operates across borders. For the music sector, harmonized data standards could make it easier for creators to control how their work is used while enabling cross-border collaboration and trade. Yet meaningful benefits will depend on continuous policy support, financial investment, and training.
Challenges facing the African music industry in the age of AI
AI’s arrival also exposes long-standing weaknesses in the region’s music infrastructure. In Nigeria,
data gaps
make it difficult to design policies that attract investment in creative industries. In South Africa, the Music Publishers Association (MPASA) has warned that
weak intellectual-property protection
already costs the industry between
R500 million and R690 million (about US$34–46 million) annually
through piracy. Without stronger regulation, AI could worsen these losses by enabling mass reproduction of styles and voices without consent or compensation.
Advertisement
Another concern is economic concentration. The bulk of profits from AI-generated African music may flow to companies controlling the technology, most of them outside the continent. If local musicians receive little return for the data or performances that train these systems, enthusiasm
could quickly erode. There are also fears that automation might undercut human creativity, reducing opportunities for session players, producers, and sound engineers.
Regional experiences from the global South
Globally, governments are struggling to regulate AI in the creative industries. Japan and Singapore now
allow copyrighted works to be mined for data
, hoping to spur innovation, while
Brazil is debating legislation
on generative AI and copyright.
South Korea excludes AI-generated music
from protection altogether. These diverging approaches suggest that a global consensus is unlikely in the near future and African countries will need their own frameworks to balance innovation with equity.
Industry practitioners like Charles Goldstuck argue that governance frameworks
are needed to protect creative labor, support innovation, and avoid exploitation while harnessing creative momentum. Without these measures, analysts warn,
musicians worldwide could lose as much as 24 percent of their revenue, or about €4 billion
by 2028, as generative models replace licensed material.
The future of music and AI in Africa
Advertisement
AI is already reshaping how music is composed, recorded, and distributed in Africa. It offers a new toolbox for creative experimentation and the rediscovery of heritage. But it also challenges existing ideas of authorship and economic value for the artist. Musicians, entrepreneurs, and policymakers will need to work together to ensure that these technologies expand opportunity rather than concentrate it.
Infrastructure and education will be critical. Investment in data storage, broadband access, and AI-literacy programs can help creative entrepreneurs participate fully in the emerging digital economy. Regional bodies such as the AfCFTA
could use trade frameworks to support these goals, promoting collaboration across borders and encouraging African-led innovation.
For musicians like Mashela and Ayo, AI is not a replacement for artistry but a new instrument, one capable of harmonizing the old and the new. If managed wisely, Africa’s fusion of heritage and technology could do more than join the global AI-music movement; it could redefine it.
